title: practical english usage - Part 66
content:

To  talk about an obligation that In statements about obligation with must the obligation most often comes from 1 Where     does    the   obligation        come     from?

75 have      (got)   to   and   must     _________ I gotta call home. A man's gotta do what a man's gotta do.

Note the spelling gotta, sometimes used in informal American English (for He'll have to /'hæftə/  get a new passport soon.

Have to is often pronounced /'hæftə/.

3  pronunciation          of  have      to;  gotta For more details of the use of do-forms and got-forms of have, ►    24.

I'm having to work very hard at the moment.

Progressive forms are possible to talk about temporary continued obligation.

I usually have to be at work at eight, (     I've usually got to-...) Have got to is not normally used to talk about repeated obligation.

When have you (got) to be back?

When do you have to be back?

present-tense auxiliary-verb forms in an informal style.

and negatives), or like an auxiliary verb (without do). Got is usually added to In this structure, have can be used like an ordinary verb (with do in questions 2 grammar:      with    or  without      do;  got Sorry, I've got to go now. Do you often have to travel on business?

necessary to do. The  meaning is quite similar to must; for differences, ►    75.1.

We   can use have (got) + infinitive to talk about obligation: things that it is 1  meaning:      obligation,       certainty 74 strong     obligation:       have      (got)   to grammar •  76  weaker obligation: should and ought to not Could you move your head a bit? I can't see. (     You should move-...)   -» Should and ought to are not used in polite requests.

Should I go and see the police, do you think?

form of shall (►  80).

In questions, should is used to ask for advice or instructions, like a less definite He ought to get a medal for living with her.

Applications must be sent...) Applications should be sent before December 30th. (More polite than You shouldn't say things like that to Granny.

You should/ought to see 'Daughter of the Moon' - it's a great film.

frequent than ought to, especially in negatives and questions.

and to say what we  think it is right for people to do. Should is much more They  are both used to talk about obligation and duty, to give and ask for advice, They ought to be more sensible, shouldn't they?

Should and ought to are very similar, and can often replace each other.

76 weaker      obligation:       should       and   ought       to For have to and have got to, ►    74.   For the use of got with have, ►    24.5  24.6.

Will need to can be used in the same way (►  532.4).

You can borrow my car, but you'll have to bring it back before ten.

sound less like direct orders from the speaker.

Will have to can be used to ‘ distance’  die instructions (►  311),  making them You can borrow my car, but you must  bring it back before ten.

Must can be used to give orders or instructions for the future.

We're going to have to repair that window.

Going to have to is also possible.

I've got to go for a job interview tomorrow.

When you leave school you'll have to find a job.

already been made. This is normal in American English (which Have to can also be used to talk about obligation coming from the speaker or Do you have to wear a tie at work? (Is there a regulation?) for you?) - Must you wear dirty old jeans all the time? (Is it personally important Catholics have to go to church on Sundays. (Their religion tells them to.) (I am telling you to.) - You really must  go to church next Sunday - you haven't been for ages.
